step_by_step_thinking:
I would like to add one point to support our agreement with Option B (recurrence being less than 10%). Although multifocality does contribute to the risk of recurrence, recent advancements in diagnostic imaging and microsurgical techniques have improved the precision of resections. This enhanced ability to achieve clearer margins has been associated with lower recurrence rates in some cases. Furthermore, surveillance protocols following initial treatment often catch recurrences early enough to manage effectively without necessitating further major interventions. Given these improvements, suggesting a slightly lower threshold below 10% might be justified under ideal conditions where meticulous attention is paid to both diagnosis and surgical technique. Thus, my revised stance supports choosing option B over other percentages indicated by Options C and D, recognizing contemporary advances in management strategies.

Therefore, my final answer remains consistent with the group’s earlier decision: **Option B (<10%)**. Based on updated understanding incorporating modern practices and technologies used today, this percentage appears more accurate reflecting real-world scenarios better compared to broader estimates suggested by Options C and D. Final
